species: Winter Storm Hoh -- all four acoustic layers simultaneously
sound_type: Four-layer simultaneous: (1) Rain 1000-16000 Hz omnidirectional on all surfaces; (2) Wind 20-4000 Hz vertical cascade from canopy with trunk flex sub-bass 20-100 Hz; (3) Hoh River + creeks 100-4000 Hz constant hydraulic; (4) Canopy 20-8000 Hz structural voice including branch fall (descending 2-6 sec signature) and snag fall (100+ dB). Combined: 20-16000 Hz full audible spectrum. Combined amplitude during storm: 95-105 dB. Duration: sustained for hours to days during major atmospheric river events. THE MOST COMPLETE ACOUSTIC DOCUMENTATION IN THE ENGINE.
ecosystem: Hoh Rainforest, Hoh River valley, Olympic National Park, Olympic Peninsula, Washington State. Annual precipitation 140-170 inches. UNESCO World Heritage Site. Sitka Spruce (Picea sitchensis) + Western Hemlock (Tsuga heterophylla) + Western Red Cedar (Thuja plicata) + Big Leaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum). Club moss (Selaginella oregana) layer as acoustic modifier. Mt. Olympus (7965 ft) as drainage headwater. Glaciers: Blue, Hoh, White.
season: Winter (November-March). Pacific storm track perpendicular to valley orientation. Atmospheric river events: 3-8 inches rain in 24-48 hours. Wind at valley floor 30-50 mph; gusts 60+ mph.
milestone: THE MOST COMPLETE PNW SOUNDSCAPE IN THE ENGINE. Only entry documenting all four primary acoustic layers simultaneously at full storm intensity. Full audible spectrum 20-16000 Hz. E=8 through completeness, not through single-source peak intensity. One Square Inch of Silence (Gordon Hempton): same location, opposite acoustic condition. Branch fall signature: the canopy's most identifiable acoustic event. 140-170 inches annual precipitation: among highest in contiguous US.

coordinates.description: Hoh Rain Forest visitor center: 47.860°N 123.934°W. Upper Hoh Road terminus. Interior wilderness (Hall of Mosses and beyond): deeper into the valley, minimum road noise. One Square Inch of Silence reference: near Three Lakes trail junction, Hoh Rainforest interior.

acoustic_data.layer_1_rain: 1000-16000 Hz. Surfaces: moss (soft, low amplitude), bark (medium, muffled), fern (percussive), standing water (ring 4000-12000 Hz), maple leaves (crisp, high amplitude Oct-Nov). Rain on saturated Hoh moss: different acoustic character from all other rain surfaces.
acoustic_data.layer_2_wind: 20-4000 Hz. Sitka Spruce crown aerodynamics 300-800 Hz. Trunk flex 20-100 Hz (felt before heard at 3m diameter). Gust frequency 5-15 seconds. Aeolian tones from hollow snags possible. Wind-driven rain angle change modulates rain layer.
acoustic_data.layer_3_river: 100-4000 Hz dominant 200-800 Hz. Main channel broadband roar. Tributaries: each with own pitch (gradient and volume dependent). Log jam dynamics: low-frequency percussion during storm. 4-8 hour hydrological lag: rain peak before river discharge peak.
acoustic_data.layer_4_canopy: 20-8000 Hz. Trunk flex: 20-60 Hz at 60m tall 3m diameter Sitka Spruce -- felt as ground vibration. Branch fall: 2-6 second descending signature (snap 1000-4000 Hz -> cascade impacts -> ground thud 100-500 Hz). Snag fall: 100+ dB at close range. Crown contact during gusts: broadband percussive event.
acoustic_data.combined: Full spectrum 20-16000 Hz. Storm intensity: 95-105 dB at forest interior measurement points. Duration: sustained hours to days. E=8 through completeness: no single layer reaches E=8 alone; four layers together cover the full spectrum with sufficient intensity across all registers.
acoustic_data.e8_calibration: E=8 through acoustic completeness rather than single-source peak intensity. Contrast: tidal bore (degree 323) E=8 through sustained hydraulic mass; rock avalanche (degree 322) E=8 through peak impulsive energy; Hoh winter storm E=8 through simultaneous complete spectrum occupation. The Hoh E=8 is the most complete acoustic event in the post-summit SPS sequence.

nodes.title: Four-Layer Completeness -- The Most Complete PNW Soundscape
nodes.description: No other entry in the engine documents all four primary acoustic layers of the temperate rainforest simultaneously at storm intensity. The Hoh winter storm is the acoustic summation of everything the Pacific Northwest natural environment produces. E=8 through completeness.

nodes.title: Branch Fall -- The Canopy's Descending Acoustic Signature
nodes.description: A large branch (20-50 kg, 4-8m long) falling through the full canopy from 50-80m height: snap (1000-4000 Hz) -> cascade impacts through layers -> ground thud (100-500 Hz). 2-6 seconds total. A uniquely identifiable descending acoustic signature. The canopy's most dramatic individual acoustic event.

nodes.title: E=8 Through Completeness vs. E=8 Through Intensity
nodes.description: The Hoh winter storm E=8 is different from single-source E=8 events. Rock avalanche: E=8 through brief peak intensity. Tidal bore: E=8 through sustained hydraulic mass. Steller Sea Lion: E=8 through anatomical mass. Hoh winter storm: E=8 through simultaneous complete spectrum occupation. The most complex E=8 calibration in the post-summit sequence.

nodes.title: Club Moss as Acoustic Modifier
nodes.description: Selaginella oregana and associated club mosses covering every surface in the Hoh Rainforest absorb high-frequency reflections, reducing reverberation time and giving the forest an intimate acoustic character. Saturated winter moss produces its own sub-layer: thin water sheets over sphagnum, the sound of moss runoff between individual drops.

cross_references.relationship: E=7 + E=8 = E=15. Founding-moment grunge vs. most complete PNW soundscape. Human acoustic contribution vs. natural acoustic ecology at full expression.
E=9 vs. E=8. Storm surge: single meteorological-oceanographic event, brief peak. Hoh winter storm: sustained multi-layer completeness. The Sound's E=9 maximum vs. the Forest's E=8 completeness.
Consecutive E=8 SPS entries. Tidal bore: single hydraulic mechanism sustained. Hoh storm: four-layer complete spectrum. Both E=8 through different mechanisms.
Three biological layers E=7. Four meteorological-ecological layers E=8. The Hoh adds the fourth layer and one unit of energy to the multi-layer simultaneous principle.

Post-summit acoustic range documented at both ends: E=7 (one bird, one note, fog, the quiet) vs. E=8 (four layers, full storm, all channels open). The Hoh winter storm is the loudest; the Varied Thrush is the quietest.
